finding_1_bias.title: Co-retrieval learns retrieval frequency, not semantic relatedness
finding_1_bias.description: The top co-retrieved pair (46x) consists of two conversation-opening memories ('Hey how are you doing' + 'Hey! How are you doing'). These appear in nearly every query because all 50 DMR queries start with 'Hey, remember that time...'. The co-retrieval rule correctly identifies that these memories are frequently retrieved together, but this reflects query structure bias, not cognitive relatedness.
finding_1_bias.impact: High-frequency 'hub' memories dominate activation, similar to Phase 1's entity-edge problem but at a smaller scale
finding_1_bias.evidence: Top 3 co-retrieval pairs all involve the same 2-3 opening memories, appearing 34-46 times

finding_2_signal_in_low_bonus.title: Lower bonus values show more semantic specificity
finding_2_signal_in_low_bonus.description: Activation events with bonus 0.04-0.10 tend to involve content-specific memories (cafe+bands for music queries, bike riding for outdoor queries). These represent genuine co-retrieval patterns within specific topic clusters.
finding_2_signal_in_low_bonus.impact: The cap (0.15) saturates high-frequency pairs, while lower bonuses preserve differentiation for meaningful relationships
finding_2_signal_in_low_bonus.evidence: Sample #23 (bonus=0.045): music query -> cafe with local bands memory. Sample #34 (bonus=0.094): music instrument query -> bike ride memory (weaker but topical)

finding_3_cap_problem.title: Activation cap (0.15) creates artificial ceiling
finding_3_cap_problem.description: Many memories receive bonus=0.1500 (the cap). This means the booster cannot distinguish between 'very relevant' and 'moderately relevant' among high-co-retrieval pairs.
finding_3_cap_problem.impact: The 22 distinct bonus values are mostly in the 0.09-0.15 range. True differentiation would show a wider spread.
finding_3_cap_problem.recommendation: Either raise the cap, or use a logarithmic/sigmoid mapping instead of linear clamping

finding_4_diversity_filter_works.title: Context diversity filter prevents graduation of single-context pairs
finding_4_diversity_filter_works.description: Of 1372 hypotheses, only 144 (10.5%) graduated. The diversity requirement (3 distinct contexts) filters out pairs that only co-occur in one query type.
finding_4_diversity_filter_works.impact: The immune system is working - 89.5% of hypotheses are rejected. However, the DMR query structure ('Hey, remember...') creates artificial diversity (different query suffixes count as different contexts even when they retrieve the same opening memories).
finding_4_diversity_filter_works.recommendation: Context hash should be based on query semantic content, not just query index

precision_estimate.method: Manual inspection of 30 sampled activation events
precision_estimate.meaningful: Approximately 30-40% of sampled events show clear semantic relatedness between query and activated memory
precision_estimate.weak: Approximately 40-50% show topical overlap but no strong cognitive relation
precision_estimate.noise: Approximately 15-20% appear to be pure retrieval frequency artifacts (opening memories activated for unrelated queries)
precision_estimate.note: This is a rule-based generator using only co-retrieval. LLM proposer (Phase 1c) should improve precision by adding semantic relation detection.

conclusion.summary: The edge ecology mechanism is structurally sound (0.5% density, 22 distinct bonuses, 10.5% survival rate) but the co-retrieval rule alone produces edges with moderate precision (~30-40% clearly meaningful). The main precision killer is retrieval frequency bias: memories that appear in many queries (like conversation openings) form edges with everything.
conclusion.next_step: Phase 1c: Add LLM proposer to generate semantic hypotheses (explains, supports, predicts). These should have higher precision than co-retrieval because they require the LLM to identify a cognitive relation, not just statistical co-occurrence.
conclusion.fix_before_1c: 1) Fix context hash to use semantic similarity, not query index. 2) Adjust cap to allow wider bonus spread. 3) Add frequency penalty: memories that appear in >50% of queries should have their co-activation edges discounted.
